meta.title: Consequential Actions — minimal
meta.author: dknauss
meta.description: The same reauth challenge guards every account-takeover action: change your password, create a user, or promote one to Administrator — each demands a fresh password confirmation before it commits.

steps.command: wp user create targetuser target@example.test --role=subscriber --user_pass=targetpass --display_name='Target User'

steps.data:
'Demo 1 — change your password. Set a New Password and click Update Profile. A dialog asks for your current password (password) before it saves, and re-challenges every time. Log out and back in to prove it changed.', 'user' => 'Demo 2 — create a user. Creating a user is a consequential action too, so the same challenge guards it.', 'users' => 'Demo 3 — promote to Administrator. Edit targetuser and set Role to Administrator to see the same challenge.', 'user-edit' => 'Demo 3 — promote to Administrator. Change this user\'s Role to Administrator and Update. Gated the same way — and notice you never need their password, only your own recent sign-in. That is the point: the challenge guards the action, not one field.', ); if ( isset( $msg[ $s->id ] ) ) { echo '

' . wp_kses_post( $msg[ $s->id ] ) . '

'; } } );
